coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of folic acid conjugated photoluminescent carbon quantum dots with ultrahigh quantum yield for targeted cancer cell fluorescence imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Saljoughi, Hamidreza
Khakbaz, Faeze
Mahani, Mohamad
coredata.dc:description: Folic acid functionalized carbon quantum dots (FA-CQDs) with ultrahigh quantum yield (50 %) were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal route using citric acid. The synthesized CQDs were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and X-ray diffraction. The cell viability of about 95 % and 97 % were obtained for MTT assay of the CQDs and FA-CQDs toward MCF-7 cells after 24 h of incubation respectively. The FA-CQDs were successfully applied for targeted imaging of ovarian cancer (type HeLa) and human breast adenocarcinoma (type MCF7) cells using fluorescence microscope.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultra high quantum yield CQDs
Targeted cancer imaging
HeLa cancer cell
MCF-7 cancer cell
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and modification of carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence imaging of HeLa and MCF-7 cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of synthesized carbon quantum dots.
Size distribution of synthesized carbon quantum dots immediately and 6 months after synthesis.
X-ray diffraction patterns of the carbon quantum dots.
FT-IR spectra of folic acid modified carbon quantum dots (lower spectrum) compared to carbon quantum dots (upper spectrum).
Electronic absorption spectra of carbon quantum dots.
Photoluminescence spectra of carbon quantum dots in 320, 330, 340, 350, 360 and 370 nm excitation wavelength.
Cytotoxicity of folic acid functionalized CQDs and CQDs. MCF-7 cells were treated in triplicates with increasing concentrations and incubated for 24 h.
Fluorescent microscope image of MCF-7 cells (A, C) and HeLa cells (B, D) that were treated with modified CQDs and CQDs respectively, λexc = 350 nm and magnification is 20×.
Comparison between this work and reported works on cancer cell imaging using carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of folic acid conjugated photoluminescent carbon quantum dots with ultrahigh quantum yield for targeted cancer cell fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiroft University of Medical Sciences, Jiroft, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiroft University of Medical Sciences, Jiroft, Iran.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of chemistry, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Folic acid functionalized carbon quantum dots (FA-CQDs) with ultrahigh quantum yield (50 %) were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal route using citric acid. The synthesized CQDs were characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and X-ray diffraction. The cell viability of about 95 % and 97 % were obtained for MTT assay of the CQDs and FA-CQDs toward MCF-7 cells after 24 h of incubation respectively. The FA-CQDs were successfully applied for targeted imaging of ovarian cancer (type HeLa) and human breast adenocarcinoma (type MCF7) cells using fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, there has been an increasing interest in the expansion of photoluminescent nanomaterials as a contrast agent (CA) for optical bio-imaging [1]. Generally, the CAs should be biocompatible, bright, nontoxic and stable against photo bleaching [2]. Most CAs based on nanoparticles are subdivided into quantum dots (QDs) and dye-doped nanoparticle QDs [3,4]. Quantum dots are nanocrystalline semiconductors with dimensions smaller than exciton Bohr radius [5,6]. Quantum dots offer several advantages such as photochemical stability, resistance to quenching, broadband excitation and narrow emission [7,8]. Among common metallic quantum dots, cadmium selenide show improved performance in various particle sizes, bare or their core-shell configurations (specially CdSe/ZnS) [9]. These advantages come with a penalty. The CdSe based QDs are among the most toxic QDs even at relatively low concentrations [10–14]. Therefore, many types of research are conducted on the synthesis of nontoxic fluorescent nanomaterials.

Carbon-based materials such as nanotube [15], graphene [16], and fullerene [17] and their new member, carbon quantum dot, have attracted considerable attention in promising applications such as sensors [18], electronic and photoelectronic devices [19], drug delivery and bioimaging [20,21]. Low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, good water solubility, outstanding photoluminescence properties, large Stokes shift and ease of functionalization are the key features that cause in the substitution of metallic quantum dots by CQDs [10]. To date, CQDs have been made using various synthesis methods including thermal carbonization [22], microwave [23] or laser treatment [24], hydrothermal [25] and solvothermal [26] routes, electrochemical [27] and chemical [28] methods.

Many types of research have been conducted on carbon dots as a contrast agent for optical bioimaging. However, there have been two challenging subjects for cell labeling imaging using CQDs. The preparation of carbon dots through the common routs usually contain low quantum yield CQDs, expensive precursors, and complex synthesis procedure. So, it is essential to design a one-pot, cost-effective and facile route for the synthesis of high quantum yield CQDs [29]. The other challenge is the selectivity of the carbon quantum dots toward cancer and normal cells. Overexpressed folate receptors on cancer cells have been used for this purpose [30].

Herein, a simple, one-step and a low cost hydrothermal procedure was developed for the synthesis of high quantum yield water dispersible CQDs from citric acid and folic acid. This efficient method was developed and applied for fast detection of the cancers.

Citric acid, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, Na2HPO4.2H2O, and K2HPO4 were obtained from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany). Folic acid, 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl) - 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and quinine hemisulfate were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Saint Louis, Missouri, USA).

The mean diameter and morphology of as-prepared CQDs studied by TEM (Hitachi, Japan). Zeta sizer NanoZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, Worcestershire, UK) was used for characterization of the zeta potential and size distribution of CQDs at a temperature of 25⁰C. The functional groups were qualified using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Bruker TENSOR 27, Germany). The diffraction pattern of the carbon quantum dots was recorded by X-ray diffraction (Bruker AXS, Cu Kα radiation, Germany). The electronic absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CQDs were recorded by carry Eclipse spectrofluorimeter (Varian, Australia) and carry50 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia) respectively. The microscopy image was taken with a fluorescent microscope (ZEISS, Axioplan 2 imaging, Germany). The Herolab Higen centrifuge (AF72.2 fixed angle rotor, angle = 40, V max = 1.5–2.2 ml, R max = 113−126 mm, K-factor = 322–366, revolutions per minute max = 21,000, Herolab, Wiesloch, Baden-Württemberg, Germany) was used through the work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and modification of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The folic acid functionalized carbon dots were synthesized through a modified one-step hydrothermal route. Two grams citric acid was dissolved in 20 ml distilled water. 0.02 g folic acid were added while stirring to form a clear yellow solution. The obtained solution was transferred to a 40 ml high-pressure reactor autoclave for six hours reaction at 200 °C. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The solution of CQDs was centrifuged at 21,000 g for 30 min and stored in a dark place at 4 °C.

The quantum yields of the carbon quantum dots were computed by slope method [31], where the quinine hemisulfate solution (dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid; quantum yield = 0.54) was used as a reference. The electronic absorption intensity of the CQDs and quinine sulfate solution for five different concentrations in 360 nm and 310 nm wavelength were determined using UV–vis spectrophotometer respectively. The corresponding photoluminescence spectra were recorded in 450 nm for same solutions using the 310 nm and 360 nm as excitation wavelength for quinine sulfate and CQDs respectively. The photoluminescence intensities were regressed on the absorbance for five solutions of each material. Finally, the follows equation was used to determine the quantum yield:
Qc =Qr (mc/mr) (ƞ2 c/ƞ2 r)
Where Q is the quantum yield, m is the measured slope of the curves. The subscript r and c are referring to reference and carbon quantum dots, respectively. ηc is the refractive index of the water (solvent of CQDs), and ηr is the refractive index of 0.1 M sulfuric acid (the solvent of quinine sulfate) that the both of them is 1.33.

The cytotoxicity of CQDs to MCF-7 cells was appraised by a typical colorimetric assay. MCF-7 cells were cultured at ten thousand cells per well density with RPMI supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/ streptomycin in 5 % carbon dioxide at 37 °C overnight. Varied concentrations of prepared CQDs solution were loaded to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Every concentration has three duplications. The culture media were abandoned, and to each well 20 μL of 5 mg/ml 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl) - 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide solution was added. the supernatant, after four hours incubation time at 37 °C was discarded. The formazan was dissolved by addition of DMSO to each well (150 μL). The mixture was shaken about 15 min. The microplate reader was used for determination of optical density at 570 nm. The equation:
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y
r a t e =
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
t h e
c e l l s
t h a t
w e r e
t r e a t e d
w i t h
c a r b o n
q u a n t u m
d o t s
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
t h e
c o n t r o l
g r o u p s
× 100
was used for determination of cell viability rate.

Human ovarian cancer HeLa cells and MCF-7 breast cancer cells were cultured with DMEM and RPMI medium containing 10 % (v/v) heat inactive FBS, and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin solution respectively (in the presence of 5 % CO2 at 37 °C to allow 80 % confluence in 24 h). After washing cells with 37 °C PBS, they were incubated with fresh media for an hour. The culture medium was replaced with a cellular medium containing 100 μg/L modified and bare CQDs for 10, 30 and 50 min at 37 °C. The excess carbon quantum dots were eliminated by triple washing with PBS. The fluorescent images were acquired with a fluorescent microscope.

To investigated the stability during the time, the size distribution of modified CQDs was studied after six months, the mean hydrodynamic-diameter of the carbon quantum dots does not change meaningfully even after this time.

Cytotoxicity of carbon quantum dot was investigated by MTT assay as a common method [33]. The MCF-7 cells were treated in triplicates with 4 different concentrations of CQDs and modified CQDs for 24 h. There was no morphological damage to the MCF-7 cells. Results showed that the cell viability was about 95 % and 97 % in the highest concentration of the CQDs and modified CQDs respectively. For further review, the potency of cytotoxic effect was represented by IC50 value calculated using extrapolation analysis. If the linearity in Fig. 7 in remain for high concentration, IC50 for CQDs and folic acid functionalized CQDs were extrapolated 743 μg/L and 1314.5 μg/L respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of HeLa and MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: A membrane linked glycoprotein (glycosylphosphatidylinositol) with 38−40 kDa molecular weight act as Folate receptor (FR) due to its high affinity toward folate [34]. Frequently, receptors are overexpressed in several types of human cancerous cells including 93 % of cases tested in ovarian (total member is 56), 91 % in endometrial (11), 80 % in brain metastases derived from epithelial cancers (5), 50 % in renal cells (18), 33 % in lung (18), 22 % in colorectal (27), 20 % in breast (53) and 14 % neuroendocrine carcinomas (21), and the level of them increase as the stage of the cancer increase while the receptor is generally absent or only very low abundant in most normal tissues [35]. Folic acid binds FRs with high affinity, and folic acid conjugated fluorescent nanoparticles such as metallic nanoclusters, or quantum dots provide tools for cancer cell imaging.

In this work, folic acid functionalized CQDs were used as a positive probe and CQDs as a negative control probe. In experiments, carbon quantum dots and their conjugations with folic acid were incubated with MCF-7 and HeLa cells for 10, 30 and 50 min and fluorescence imaging were performed after removing the unbounded probe. Fig. 8 displays the fluorescence microscopic image of HeLa and MCF-7 cells after 10-minute treatment with 100 μg/mL carbon quantum dots and their conjugations with folic acid. It seems that FA-CQDs completely entered into MCF-7 (Fig. 8A) and HeLa (Fig. 8B) cells and display bright photoluminescence, while the CQDs rarely entered in cells in this time.

Table 1 displays some published applications of CQDs in cellular imaging [29,30,36–39]. Obviously, the targeting power of folic acid is combined with high quantum yield power and negligible cytotoxicity of CQDs for fast cancer cell imaging.

High quantum yield folic acid functionalized carbon quantum dots (FA-CQDs) were synthesized through a simple one-step method. The FA-CQDs were evaluated for cell viability and effectively used for targeting and imaging of cervical cancer and breast adenocarcinoma cells. The conjugation with folic acid molecules increases the selective internalization of FA-CQDs into cancer cells in comparison to normal cells, due to over-expression of folate receptors.

All the authors contribute in different part of the works (idea, experiments, data collection, discussion and manuscript preparation). All the authors are employed at university where research or education is the primary function of the entity.

This work has been financially supported by the research deputy of Jiroft University of Medical Sciences (This support is only for supporting research of faculty members). The grant number was p-94-34.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been financially supported by the Research Deputy of Jiroft University of Medical Sciences. The grant number was p-94-34.
